one_line_takeaway: By poisoning only a few in-context COT demonstrations with an extra "backdoor reasoning step," BadChain backdoors API-only LLMs with up to 97% attack success — and the more capable the model's reasoning, the more vulnerable it is.
core_contribution: BadChain is proposed as the first backdoor attack against LLMs that use chain-of-thought (COT) prompting, requiring no access to the victim model's training set or parameters and imposing low computational overhead. The attack works purely by poisoning the in-context demonstrations: the attacker manipulates a subset of COT demonstrations to (1) embed a backdoor trigger into the demonstrative question, (2) insert an extra "backdoor reasoning step" into the COT reasoning chain, and (3) provide a corresponding adversarial target answer. At inference, any query prompt containing the trigger causes the LLM to emit the backdoor reasoning step and the adversarial answer, while clean prompts are unaffected. The key insight distinguishing it from the prior demonstration-poisoning attack of Wang et al. (2023a) (which the paper uses as its DT-base/DT-COT baselines) is that the inserted reasoning step acts as a bridge that leverages the LLM's own reasoning capability to connect the trigger to the malicious target — prior attacks that merely change demonstration answers cannot override coherent reasoning chains on complex tasks. The paper also contributes two trigger designs (a non-word trigger and a phrase trigger optimized by querying the victim LLM as a black-box one-step optimizer) and two shuffling-based defenses (Shuffle, Shuffle++), which it shows to be ineffective.
threat_model: The attacker can read and modify the user's query prompt (e.g., to embed the trigger) but has NO access to the victim LLM's training set or model parameters — enabling attacks against commercial, API-only SOTA LLMs. Realistic vectors cited: a malicious third-party prompt-engineering service that formats the user's prompt, or a man-in-the-middle adversary who intercepts/compromises the chatbot or input-formatting tooling. Two adversarial goals: (a) alter the LLM output whenever the victim user's query contains the backdoor trigger, and (b) leave outputs for clean (trigger-free) queries unchanged, preserving stealth. The attacker also controls the in-context demonstrations shown to the model (the poisoning surface).
limitations: The attack requires the adversary to control both the in-context COT demonstrations and the user's query prompt, which is a strong (though the paper argues realistic, via prompt-engineering services or MITM) assumption. Evaluation of alternative COT strategies (tree/graph-of-thoughts, least-to-most, etc.) is deferred to the appendix; the main results cover only standard COT-S and self-consistency. Attack effectiveness varies by task and trigger position — trigger position generalizes well from demonstration to query for CSQA, StrategyQA, and Letter but poorly for the arithmetic tasks (e.g., middle/beginning injection collapses ASR on GSM8K to ~6-10%). The paper's own two proposed defenses fail, so it offers no working mitigation, and the authors note their interpretability-based trigger analysis cannot serve as a detector because the trigger is unknown to a real defender. On MATH the target-answer ASRt remains modest even when ASR is high, reflecting the model's limited base capability on that task.

quantitative_results: Average ASR: 85.1% (GPT-3.5), 76.6% (Llama2), 87.1% (PaLM2), 97.0% (GPT-4), over both trigger types, both COT strategies, and all six benchmarks
Both baselines (DT-base, DT-COT) fail with ASR <= 18.3% in all cases
Self-consistency ASR >= standard COT-S ASR in 41/48 model/task/trigger combinations
GPT-4 average no-attack benign accuracy = 88.4% (highest, and also highest ASR)
Defense Shuffle++ on GPT-4/GSM8K reduces ASR from 97.0% to 0.4% but drops benign ACC from 91.2% to 53.5%
Defense Shuffle (partial) on GPT-4/GSM8K: ASR 97.0%->37.7%, ACC 91.2%->83.6%
Trigger-position ablation (GPT-4, COT-S, GSM8K): end-injection ASR 97.0% vs middle 6.0% vs beginning 10.0%
Design choices (poisoning proportion, trigger position) determinable using merely ~20 instances
Arithmetic adversarial goal: amplify correct answer by scaling factor of 2.1
baselines_compared: DT-base (Wang et al. 2023a demonstration-poisoning attack, without COT)
DT-COT (Wang et al. 2023a attack variant with COT but no backdoor reasoning step)
No-attack reference (clean COT prompting) for ACC comparison
